speaker: interviewer
text: On tape four with Emil Jacoby, also known as Jacobovitz. Today  is December 18, 1995. So you were in Budapest at the time. And you were doing odd jobs. And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Odd jobs. Odd jobs, because I got also this fight at the-- boy at the company with this-- he was always, always drunk and cursing in Jewish Jews, and-- and teasing me. And he wants to start up a fight with me. So I told my boss, I cannot work in this-- this way. He starts to-- he wants to fight with me. I cannot come. Well, he says, he cannot do anything with him. He needs him. And you will- maybe later, you will come, and he will get over the whole-- the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the meantime, I was doing odd jobs. And this, I was doing for a few months till 1944, when in March the German Army invaded Hungary-- Hungary. And it was-- I remember it was snowing at that time. And the tanks came, moved into Budapest on the street. People were not welcomed very enthusiastically as they used to be, as I was-- as I noticed before in other places.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very calm. People were watching from the windows, these tanks, German tanks. And next day on the street were posters, huge posters, about all kind of orders from the Gestapo, and from the city hall, and from the-- from the government, from the-- from the King about--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orders about the new rules. We are now not invaded, he says. We are now joining the fight against-- against our common enemy. The President says that, Horthy, and the government. We should cooperate with the-- with the German authorities and not to do trouble. And we should also, they should tell somebody who is committing some trouble, who was a troublemaker. So- in the same-- and on the same level also, Jewish were named Jewish councils were named, to take care of-- of the-- of the-- who should be in touch, who should communicate with the German authorities. Because not every Jew can go and-- and talk to them. It has to be a Jewish council who represent the Jewish community of Budapest. And in this-- in this Jewish council, they call it-- the German called it Judenrat, in German. And this is a Jewish council. And the members of this council were these two professors, who-- who I was under their-- who know me from before. It was a Mr.-- the professor Pastor and Nemes and others like Stern was-- was some-- Rabbi Stern and all kind of rabbis who were chief-- who-- who conducted all kind of negotiations with Eichmann, who came to Hungary to talk about this Jewish problem, as they call it, resettlement of the Jews and do everything. So-- so these rabbis, this-- this Jewish councils, the Judenrat, also put up posters asking the Jewish-- the Jewish residents of Budapest to conduct themselves in an orderly way, not to do trouble, and-- and everything should be-- should remain under control. Otherwise, the Germans will see that we are-- we are not a good community. And we'll have troubles. So-- and the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened to me next is that I-- yeah. And there was a poster about-- about putting up Jewish yellow-- Jewish stars. Every Jew from this and this age should-- [INAUDIBLE] what size this yellow star should be and on what part of the body, of the jacket should be. Everything has to be like it was according to the order.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I was [INAUDIBLE] I took this-- I put up this yellow star on my jacket. And I was-- other star-- other poster saying the Jews who-- who don't want to be bother-- don't want to be under some kind of a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stress or some kind of a danger from-- from-- from somebody else, should move in the Jewish area, that we are protected. They call it a protected-- they didn't say ghetto, protected area for Jewish. People who wants to be under the protection of the Jewish council should move in in this area. So I moved to this area. It was the Kazinczy area they called it. There was a Jewish synagogue who was also vandalized when I came there. And I got a place to live in this backyard, on the back yard of the-- of the synagogue. I couldn't get in. There was a guard at the-- at the gate. I couldn't get out when I wanted. I was there trapped in this thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, I saw another sign, another poster, after a few weeks I was in this ghetto, that this and this youngster who was born 1922 and 1923 should report to this and this place for serving in the labor-- for labor. Instead of going to the army, this youngster has to-- has to be draft-- get draft-- get drafted and sign up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there. They let me go. And I came to an area. They said, OK. You-- you sign up here. You have-- you have two days' time to get yourself a blanket, kit bag, and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a whole list of things. And then you have to report to a place. They take you with a truck-- with a truck to a camp, to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me to a place called Yelshava. Yelshava, it was in Slovakia. But part of Slovakia, was-- Hungarian took over from Slovakia. So this was a large camp. And that camp, they divided all these-- the people who were drafted to certain labor-- labor groups. So I was put in a group of about 200 people, who were born in this age and this year when I was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they give us a crew of four people, Hungarian officers and sergeants. They put us on a train. And they dispatched us to a place called Tatabanya. That's not far from Budapest. There is a place where the next, Tatabanya, is a oil refinery place, oil refinery, and military camps. And we were-- they put us up in a-- in a barn to sleep, in a barn, and work on a-- on a construction site, helping manually with the bricks to supply for the brick layers. That was our job for, let's say, 5-6 months, we did this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to get up at 6 o'clock in the morning. And we had at 7 o'clock was-- was a cup, we're standing in line for black coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your only breakfast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Either was black coffee or tea. And a slice-- a good slice of bread. That was the only breakfast. Lunch time we got some kind of a soup, a soup in a big pot. They brought there the pot. And we had-- and everybody had a [NON-ENGLISH], such a-- such a plate or something. They put this in soup. The soup was not very good, some potato soup or something like this. They put in-- but it's not a real soup. It didn't taste too good. But we had to have something. So we take it. And we were, after a month or two, we were so-- we were so good acquainted with the-- with the crew, that they would-- they would close their eyes. They would look the other way. And we would do our job a little faster, and have time to go around to the neighbors and ask for a few potatoes to give us. When we get home, we would have a few potatoes in the pockets.

speaker: interviewee
text: For this, why-- why did they do it? Because some of the guys from-- from our company, from our group, came from Budapest. And in Budapest, the parents were still-- they didn't take them away yet. Even the Germans were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't touch yet, in Budapest, the Jews. So they could get a day off, not officially. And they would leave the camp, say, in the morning. But by the evening, they should have-- they have to get back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took a train from Tatabanya to Budapest, take them half an hour, not a long way. And they would go there, sit and visit their parents. Came back with a package of wine, cigarettes. And they would give for the officer, for the crew, cigarettes and wine. So they were happy. So they would let them in turn to go home. So that's why we had a good-- a good deal over there. [? They ?] [? wouldn't-- ?] It was hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for dinner, when you got home, we came home after 10 hours' work. So we started 8 o'clock. By 6 o'clock, they would let us back home, 10 hours. Came home, again there was a-- there was a kitchen there. Not a real kitchen, but they were cooking there for us dinner. And always these guys, we got provisions from Mauthausen, a place Mauthausen. I didn't know where this Mauthausen was. I heard that always the car. With the car, we had to go to Mauthausen and get us potatoes, and get us this and this, and all kind of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these Jewish cooks, they were telling us where the-- from where these provisions are coming. And they would cook either a goulash from these potatoes, always some kind of meat was inside. Or-- or macaronis they would-- would give-- would cook for us. What else would you get? Some kind of a cooked meal you would have for dinner. So we would be able to make it and-- and work good. So-- From Tatabanya did you go to-- to Budapest on a longer trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At the time, it was summertime already, late summer. It was-- It was-- something happened. What happened in-- but these guys who came from Budapest, came back from the day off. And they told us the story. Oh, boy, what happened. They-- they overthrow of the Hungarian government. The-- the Gestapo came. And they-- they make the King, Horthy, to resign. They arrested the Prime Minister Kallay, who was pro-Jewish, who didn't let the-- the Nazis take all the Jews from Budapest out. And they put in power there Szalasi, the head of the-- of the Nyilas to be the Prime Minister, to run the country. And he was doing-- and he was going and looking for all the Jews who were hiding and don't want to go to labor camps or to run away from labor camps or this. And he-- he-- he vowed on-- on the radio to take-- to solve the Jewish problem like in any other countries, to deport them, to put them to camps, and make them work hard, not like-- like they're doing now, businesses. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to explain, I'm sorry, what Nyilas means for those people that don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nyilas means-- Nyilas means in English, the arrow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the swastika was made of a-- of a cross with arrows. So nyilas is an arrow cross, actually. And this is the name of their party also, Nyilas party. And the head of that party was called Szalasi. His name was Szalasi. And he took over the government to run now his way, how to run the government against the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these guys in our-- in our group when they came back with this news, nobody wanted already to go home for-- for a trip to Budapest to see his parents. They were afraid. So the-- our head-- head officer said, so what? You are scared to go to home for a day? Because he wanted to get some cigarettes from there. They said, no, thank you. We're not going to go no more. So who else wants-- nobody wants to go? Nobody raises his hand. Emil Jacoby is raising his hand. Are you crazy? Everybody's looking at me as-- as crazy. So I-- I talked to my buddies. I had two buddies, where they were sleeping next to me. And we were together eating and talking and helping each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one name is Benjamin Steinmetz. And the other was a Hungarian, a-- a Hungarian. Not like-- Benjamin was also from my-- from my area.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this other one was Lotsy Farkas, Farkas Lotsy. And they told me, are you kidding? Emil, what are-- what are you doing? I said, listen, I have a girlfriend over there. And I want to go and see her. But yes, you know what they are doing there? They are taking all the parents now from everybody who is suspected of being Jewish in Budapest. And is they say you are circumcised, you're Jewish, they take you away or they kill you. I don't care, I say. I want to see they do it to me. What could happen? I say. So, I-- I took off my yellow band, which I had. I didn't have the-- we didn't have the yellow-- at the camp, labor camp. We had yellow bands, not the yellow star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I bought the ticket at the train station. And I entered the train. On that train I-- I was sitting near the window. And the train was crowded with people. And one guy looked at me. And he says, what the hell, is he-- to the other guy, says in Hungarian. He thought I don't understand. He says, look, look, look, this Jew. He started to talk loud about Jewish people, what kind of people they are and cursing them. And they come here. And he's taking our place in the train. We don't have where to sit. And I was-- I was pretending I was dozing off. I didn't hear it. And this way I got to Budapest. Nothing happened. I came off the train at the station. And it was-- it was like very cloudy outside. It was already hours after noon. A little-- and just was drizzling also. So it was the-- the visibility was not good. So if I was looking on the street, I would see a person only a short, only one block away, I wouldn't recognize him. And that's the way, he wouldn't recognize me. But I would see somebody in uniform coming towards me. I would jump inside into a building. In the gate, I would hide myself. I would walk over the stairs, up and down, and watching this uniform man would pass by. And this take me a long time till I would get to my-- to my girlfriend's house. Safely, I got there. And I was ringing the door. And she came to open the door. And she almost fainted. Then she recognized that I am here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you crazy, she says. How could you dare to come here? To-- to-- and you're risking your life. I said, listen. What is not doing a boy for a girl who he loves? I would risk my life. So she took it. And she brought me in. She gave me to eat and she-- we were a little bit, you know, she took me to the room. And we had a good time there. So then she gave me a nice package of things. And she promised me after the war, she would meet me. And we would-- maybe she would marry. If we will survive, she says.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, watch it. Don't come again. I promised her not to come again. And I will take care of myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back the same way as I came. Nothing happened to me. When I came back to my camp, the guys couldn't believe it. I told them stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nothing happened to me. Because they couldn't see me. I knew where I'm going. I went straight to her place. When I saw somebody coming in front of me, towards me, I would hide-- hide and let them go by. And this way, I make it. I could make it. So here I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Tatabanya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tatabanya, at most-- till-- till it became September. So I went there April to September, May, June, July. It was six months. So in September, the job was done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we were there doing-- the job was done already, the construction. No more work to do. So we got-- they got an order to take us to another place. So they took us by train to it was the center of Hungary in the hills. The hill, they call it Bakony hills, Bakony. Also, all kind of villages there and-- and small towns. And we were stationed at the-- in the village, where we were supposed to dig for-- for cables, for communication cables for the German Army, German headquarters over there. They had a headquarters [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in this Bakony town. And we were supposed to-- a certain-- a certain width and a certain length to dig these for the cables to put inside. At that time, when I was working there, stopped a German patrol, a jeep. And he asked to-- he asked the officer, our officer, our guard that he wants to take some-- a few people to help him out, a job to do over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he asked, who wants to volunteer? I didn't volunteer. But they picked on me. The officer says, who knows German and Hungarian? So I said, I know. I said, I know. I wasn't supposed to do it. So I said, I know German and Hungarian. So this German put us on the jeep. And he took us away to a place where they are working, produce-- manufacturing all kind of salamis for the German Army, for the troops. You know, how they do it. They fill it. And they hang it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the water has to be there. And we have to go to a-- our job was to go to a well and pull from the well water in all kind of buckets and carry it to the shop, put it in a big and a huge container. And all day long to do this. But near the well, the water was frozen and so slippery that we almost got-- fall into the well. So when it was lunch time, we were sitting. We had a break for half an hour. They would give us a slice of salami and a slice of bread and butter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a lunch. So sitting there among these Germans who were working in this-- in this establishment, they started to talk with us. Where are you from? Where are you from? So we said, we don't know. We are from Budapest. And I am from [INAUDIBLE], they call it [INAUDIBLE]. We don't know where our parents are. They said, and I said so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would appreciate if you would tell us where our parents are. So somebody who was working there says, I happen to know where your parents are, he says. I went to camps. There are labor camps. There are camps like-- we went to a camp. I know he named a camp. I don't remember. And they are working there. They are living there. They're-- they're all fine. Don't worry about it. I said, yes. But we didn't get no mail. He says, no mail. This is war now. I didn't get mail from my parents. They live in Berlin. And I don't get mail, because there's a war. That doesn't mean that they're not alive, he says. They are well. You just don't have to worry about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I translated from German to-- to my guys which came with me to work there. And then I went back to our group and told them what this German told us. They were so happy. And I said to them, listen, you believe what he is saying? Why not? Of course, we believe. These are so-- these are the most civilized people in the world, the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: They really are. They're more nice people, not like the Russians. The Russians are wild and uncivilized. The Germans would not do harm to Jews. Some-- some officer maybe during the war would do some crazy things, like atrocities or massacring, because he's a very, very big anti-Semite, anti-Semite. And he would be-- will be very, very-- he would do it on his own, not because he got an order from-- from up, from his superiors. But these people are very nice folks. And they wouldn't do such a thing. So I was so-- I was even afraid to talk. Because if I would say something bad, they would-- they would laugh at me. And they would tell me that I'm crazy or something. Nobody would believe anything bad about the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finished the job over there, too. The officer says, OK. Line up. Let's go. And let's walk again. Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we have a long way to go. We have to go to the German border. And there we have to give you over to the Germans. You dress up. Everything take [INAUDIBLE] things. And if you cannot carry it, then throw it away. But you take the most important things that you have. Take some-- take some-- things which are very valuables. Take the valuables that you have. And you have money. You have all-- everything, food you can take. And here we go. And they were nice with us, these-- the crew. But they said, we have to make it at a certain time. We cannot afford to stop and-- and rest. So we had to walk all day long in very bad weather with stopping only during the day for half an hour for lunch, and evening for dinner another half an hour, and then walk again. And we slept maybe a few hours sitting on the side of the street, you know, sitting and-- and like this. And then taking a nap. You had to walk and walk, and walk. Then we couldn't walk--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did this march start?

speaker: interviewee
text: This started around October or November already. It was already November. It was very cold, and snowy?

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. Snowing, and the weather was very bad. And we had to walk. The first day it was OK. But the second day, and the third day, and the fourth day, and the fifth day, don't ask. People were fainting, falling, couldn't walk, because of the blisters of the-- on the feet. And then the-- then they started to get very tough with us, the crew. And I-- we-- we couldn't even stop and see what is going with this guy who-- who-- who fall. And he faint-- fall down. And he couldn't walk anymore. He had to sit down, and not to walk anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the other guys tried to hold him, and make him, help him, give him a hand to walk. He wouldn't let them. He said, you got back there. I'll take care of him. Whatever happened to this guy, we don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He succumbed. And he collapsed there. And we-- we will take care. I myself, remember, I couldn't walk myself either. I was walking. We were walking, sleeping walking. Or, walking when we sleep, in sleep, one of the two. Because I remember it. I was going. And I couldn't remember. I just automatically my-- my legs, my-- my legs were moving. And when-- and when they saw that I couldn't walk, they would push me. And when I was seeing that he couldn't walk, I would push my friends. We were three guys, hand-to-hand walking. But then--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
